questions.question: Which transport protocol and port does NTP use?
questions.explanation: NTP runs over UDP port 123 for clock synchronization messages.

questions.question: What does the stratum number indicate in NTP?
options: The authentication strength of the NTP server
The distance of an NTP server from the reference clock — lower is more accurate
The NTP protocol version in use
questions.explanation: Stratum measures hops from a reference clock. Stratum 0 is the reference clock itself; a lower stratum number means a more accurate source. Stratum 15 is the max usable.

questions.question: What is the stratum value of a reference clock such as an atomic clock or GPS receiver?
questions.explanation: Reference clocks (atomic, GPS) sit at stratum 0 within the NTP hierarchy. Devices directly synced to them become stratum 1.

questions.question: Which command configures a device as an NTP client of the server 10.1.1.1?
questions.explanation: 'ntp server <ip>' points the device at a specified NTP server and puts it into client mode.

questions.question: What is the default stratum assigned when you configure 'ntp master' without specifying a number?
questions.explanation: The default stratum for 'ntp master' is 8 when no value is specified.

questions.question: In 'show ntp associations', what does an asterisk (*) next to a server indicate?
options: The server is a stratum 1 source
The device is the sys.peer — the NTP server currently selected for sync
The server is configured but unreachable
questions.explanation: The asterisk marks the sys.peer: the currently selected NTP server the device is synchronizing to.

questions.question: What is the default time zone on a Cisco device?
questions.explanation: Cisco devices default to UTC (Coordinated Universal Time). Time zones are converted per device using 'clock timezone'.

questions.question: Which NTP mode peers two equal-stratum devices so they can help each other stay in sync?
questions.explanation: Symmetric active mode, configured with 'ntp peer', lets two same-stratum devices act as mutual time backups.

questions.question: Why is a loopback interface commonly used as the NTP source on a Cisco router?
options: It provides a faster NTP response
It's the only interface allowed to send NTP
It remains reachable even if a physical interface fails
questions.explanation: Loopback interfaces never go down, so using one as the NTP source keeps the server reachable even if a physical interface fails.

questions.question: Which hash algorithm does Cisco NTP authentication traditionally use?
questions.explanation: Cisco's NTP authentication uses MD5 keys configured via 'ntp authentication-key <n> md5 <key>'.

questions.question: In 'show clock detail', what does an asterisk before the time indicate?
options: The clock is authoritative and NTP-synced
The time source is the hardware calendar — the clock is NOT authoritative
The device is currently in daylight saving time
The clock was manually set by user
questions.explanation: An asterisk in 'show clock detail' marks the time as NOT authoritative (sourced from hardware calendar or user config, not NTP).

questions.question: What is the highest usable stratum value in NTP?
questions.explanation: Stratum 15 is the maximum usable value; stratum 16 and above is considered unsynchronized/unreliable.
